---
title: "Lab 3 Tutorial | PAF 516"
subtitle: "Spatial Data Integration & Environmental Justice"
format:
  html:
    theme: readable
    highlight-style: tango
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Steps"
    self-contained: true
    number-sections: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  fig-width: 10
---

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to use this file
**First time:** Click **Render** (Cmd+Shift+K Mac / Ctrl+Shift+K PC) to produce a complete HTML with all results. Then come back and run chunk by chunk to understand each step.

**Chunk by chunk:** Place cursor inside any chunk → Cmd+Return (Mac) / Ctrl+Enter (PC).

This file is for learning — do not edit it. When ready for the assignment, go to:
[https://antjam-howell.github.io/paf-516-labs/lab3-assignment.html](https://antjam-howell.github.io/paf-516-labs/lab3-assignment.html)
:::


```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE, fig.width = 10)
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
options(tigris_progress_bar = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| label: packages
# ── Package Setup (runs automatically on first knit) ─────────────────
# Downloads the exact package versions used to build this lab.
# The if-guard ensures this only runs once — no need to comment out.
if (!file.exists(".renv_done")) {
  if (!requireNamespace("renv", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("renv")
  download.file(
    url      = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/AntJam-Howell/paf-516-labs/main/Lab3/renv.lock",
    destfile = "renv.lock", mode = "wb"
  )
  renv::restore(lockfile = "renv.lock", prompt = FALSE)
  file.create(".renv_done")
}

library(tidyverse)
library(tidycensus)
library(sf)
library(tigris)
library(psych)
library(patchwork)
library(viridis)
library(lme4)         # for ICC estimation
library(performance)  # for icc() function
```

```{r}
#| label: api-key
# Get a free Census API key at: https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html
# After receiving your key by email, run ONCE in the RStudio console:
#   census_api_key("YOUR_KEY_HERE", install = TRUE)
# Then restart R: Session -> Restart R
# You only need to do this once — the key is saved to your .Renviron file.
```

```{r}
#| label: fallback-data
# ── FALLBACK: Uncomment if Census API is unavailable ──────────────────
# Option A (local — save .rds to PAF516/Lab3/data/ first):
# cbg_raw <- readRDS("../data/cbg_hardship_maricopa.rds")
#
# Option B (GitHub):
# cbg_raw <- readRDS(url("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/AntJam-Howell/paf-516-labs/main/Lab3/data/cbg_hardship_maricopa.rds"))
#
# If using fallback, skip to Step 2.
```


# Step 1: Pull Maricopa County Block Group Data

We build on the same 5-variable hardship indicator set from Labs 1 and 2, but now pull only **Maricopa County block groups** — the finest ACS geography and the foundation for neighborhood-level spatial analysis.

```{r}
#| label: step.01-vars

hardship_vars <- c(
  pov_below50    = "C17002_002",
  pov_50to99     = "C17002_003",
  pov_den        = "C17002_001",
  median_income  = "B19013_001",
  pct_unemp_num  = "B23025_005",
  pct_unemp_den  = "B23025_002",
  pct_no_hs_num  = "B15003_002",
  pct_no_hs_den  = "B15003_001",
  pct_no_ins_num = "B27010_017",
  pct_no_ins_den = "B27010_001"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: step.01-pull
#| results: hide

cbg_raw <- get_acs(
  geography    = "block group",
  variables    = hardship_vars,
  state        = "AZ",
  county       = "Maricopa",
  year         = 2023,
  survey       = "acs5",
  geometry     = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
)
```


# Step 2: Build the 5-Variable Hardship Index

Mirrors Lab 1: pivot to wide, compute rates, standardize to z-scores, reverse-code income, average into composite index.

```{r}
#| label: step.02-wrangle

cbg_wide <- cbg_raw %>%
  select(-moe) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = estimate) %>%
  mutate(
    poverty_rate = (pov_below50 + pov_50to99) / pov_den,
    unemp_rate   = pct_unemp_num / pct_unemp_den,
    no_hs_rate   = pct_no_hs_num / pct_no_hs_den,
    no_ins_rate  = pct_no_ins_num / pct_no_ins_den
  )

cbg_complete <- cbg_wide %>%
  drop_na(poverty_rate, unemp_rate, no_hs_rate, no_ins_rate, median_income)

index_vars <- cbg_complete %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(poverty_rate, unemp_rate, no_hs_rate, no_ins_rate, median_income)

index_z <- index_vars %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ as.numeric(scale(.)))) %>%
  mutate(median_income = -1 * median_income)

alpha_original <- psych::alpha(index_z)
cat("Original 5-variable alpha:", round(alpha_original$total$raw_alpha, 3), "\n")
```

```{r}
#| label: step.02-index

index_z$hardship_index <- rowMeans(index_z[, 1:5], na.rm = TRUE)
cbg_complete$hardship_index <- index_z$hardship_index
cat("CBGs with valid index:", nrow(cbg_complete), "\n")
summary(cbg_complete$hardship_index)
```


# Step 3: Aggregation Sensitivity — Does Scale Matter? (ICC)

Before doing spatial joins at the block group level, we should ask: **does using block groups vs. tracts meaningfully change our conclusions?** The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) answers this by measuring what fraction of total variance is *between* tracts vs. *within* them.

If the ICC is high, block groups within the same tract are similar — aggregating to tracts loses little. If the ICC is low, there is substantial within-tract variation that tract-level analysis would miss.

```{r}
#| label: step.03-tract-id

# Extract the tract GEOID from the block group GEOID
# Block group GEOID (15 chars): state(2) + county(3) + tract(6) + bg(1)
# First 11 characters = tract GEOID
cbg_complete <- cbg_complete |>
  mutate(tract_id = str_sub(GEOID, 1, 11))

cat("Unique tracts containing these block groups:", n_distinct(cbg_complete$tract_id), "\n")
cat("Average block groups per tract:",
    round(nrow(cbg_complete) / n_distinct(cbg_complete$tract_id), 1), "\n")
```

```{r}
#| label: step.03-icc

# Compute ICC using a one-way random effects model
# hardship_index ~ 1 + (1 | tract_id) models tracts as random grouping units
model_icc <- lmer(hardship_index ~ 1 + (1 | tract_id),
                  data = cbg_complete,
                  REML = TRUE)

icc_result <- performance::icc(model_icc)
icc_val <- round(icc_result$ICC_adjusted, 3)

cat("=== Intraclass Correlation Coefficient ===\n")
cat("ICC:", icc_val, "\n")
cat("\nInterpretation:\n")
if (icc_val > 0.75) {
  cat("HIGH ICC: Block groups within each tract are very similar.\n")
  cat("Aggregating to tract level is acceptable for most purposes.\n")
} else if (icc_val > 0.40) {
  cat("MODERATE ICC: Meaningful within-tract variation exists.\n")
  cat("Tract-level analysis is usable but suppresses some neighborhood variation.\n")
} else {
  cat("LOW ICC: Substantial within-tract variation detected.\n")
  cat("Block group geography is strongly preferred. Aggregating to tracts loses\n")
  cat("important neighborhood-level information.\n")
}
```

```{r}
#| label: step.03-aggregate-map
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6

# Aggregate block group hardship to tract level (area-weighted mean)
cbg_complete <- cbg_complete |>
  mutate(area = st_area(geometry) |> as.numeric())

tract_aggregated <- cbg_complete |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  group_by(tract_id) |>
  summarise(
    hardship_tract = weighted.mean(hardship_index, w = area, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_cbg = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Pull tract geometries to map the aggregated index
tract_geo <- get_acs(
  geography    = "tract",
  variables    = "B01001_001",
  state        = "AZ",
  county       = "Maricopa",
  year         = 2023,
  geometry     = TRUE,
  progress_bar = FALSE
) |>
  select(GEOID, geometry) |>
  left_join(tract_aggregated, by = c("GEOID" = "tract_id"))

# Side-by-side: block group vs. tract
p_cbg <- ggplot(cbg_complete) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hardship_index), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno", name = "Hardship\n(Block Group)",
                       na.value = "grey80") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Block Group Level",
       subtitle = paste0("n = ", nrow(cbg_complete), " block groups"))

p_tract <- ggplot(tract_geo) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hardship_tract), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno", name = "Hardship\n(Tract Agg.)",
                       na.value = "grey80") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Census Tract Level (Aggregated)",
       subtitle = paste0("n = ", n_distinct(tract_geo$GEOID), " tracts"))

p_cbg + p_tract +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = paste0("Scale Sensitivity: Block Group vs. Tract  |  ICC = ", icc_val),
    caption = "ACS 5-Year (2023) | Tract values = area-weighted mean of block group hardship index"
  )
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Extension for Lab 4
Once you have covered Moran's I in Module 4, a natural follow-up is to run **Moran's I on the aggregation residuals** (block group hardship − tract mean). If the residuals are spatially clustered, MAUP is introducing *systematic spatial bias* — not just random noise. This connects Fotheringham & Wong's (1991) theoretical warning to your specific dataset.
:::


# Step 4: Load External Point Data (Environmental Facilities)

We now bring in a non-Census dataset: EPA-regulated environmental facilities in Maricopa County. These are point locations that we will spatially join to block group polygons.

```{r}
#| label: step.04-facilities

# Known EPA-regulated and Superfund facilities in Maricopa County
# Sources: EPA TRI Explorer, EPA Superfund site listings
facilities <- tibble(
  name     = c(
    "APS Ocotillo Power Plant",
    "Intel Chandler Fab 32",
    "Honeywell Aerospace",
    "Univar Solutions (chemical dist.)",
    "Goodyear Airport Industrial Park",
    "Former Motorola 52nd Street Site",
    "19th Avenue Landfill",
    "Arizona Plating Works",
    "Phoenix Sky Harbor Area (TRI cluster)",
    "Mesa NW Industrial (TRI cluster)"
  ),
  type     = c(
    "Power Generation","Semiconductor Mfg","Aerospace Mfg",
    "Chemical Distribution","Industrial/Airport","Superfund",
    "Superfund/Landfill","Superfund","TRI Cluster","TRI Cluster"
  ),
  lat      = c(33.3919, 33.2974, 33.4352, 33.4423, 33.4350,
               33.4162, 33.5070, 33.4445, 33.4372, 33.4512),
  lon      = c(-111.8828, -111.8156, -112.0694, -111.9738, -112.3797,
               -111.9763, -112.1171, -112.0738, -112.0167, -111.8935)
)

cat("Facilities loaded:", nrow(facilities), "\n")
print(facilities |> select(name, type))
```


# Step 5: Convert to sf and Align CRS

Before any spatial operation, both layers must share the same CRS. We project to **EPSG:2868** (Arizona Central, US survey feet) — appropriate for accurate distance calculations in Maricopa County.

```{r}
#| label: step.05-crs

# Convert facility tibble to sf point layer (WGS84 lat/lon)
facilities_sf <- st_as_sf(facilities, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

# Project both layers to EPSG:2868 — Arizona Central (US survey feet)
# This CRS gives accurate distances for buffer analysis in Maricopa County
facilities_proj <- st_transform(facilities_sf, crs = 2868)
cbg_proj        <- st_transform(cbg_complete,  crs = 2868)

# Validate geometries
cbg_proj <- st_make_valid(cbg_proj)

cat("CRS check:\n")
cat("  Facilities:", st_crs(facilities_proj)$epsg, "\n")
cat("  Block groups:", st_crs(cbg_proj)$epsg, "\n")
cat("  Units:", st_crs(facilities_proj)$units, "\n")
```


# Step 6: Point-in-Polygon Spatial Join

Count how many EPA facilities fall within each block group — producing a `facility_count` variable we can add to the hardship index.

```{r}
#| label: step.06-spatial-join

# Spatial join: attach facility info to CBGs that contain them
cbg_with_facilities <- st_join(cbg_proj, facilities_proj, join = st_intersects)

# Count facilities per CBG
facility_counts <- cbg_with_facilities |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  group_by(GEOID) |>
  summarise(
    facility_count = sum(!is.na(name)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Merge counts back to the projected CBG layer
cbg_proj <- cbg_proj |>
  left_join(facility_counts, by = "GEOID") |>
  mutate(facility_count = replace_na(facility_count, 0))

cat("Block groups with at least one facility:", sum(cbg_proj$facility_count > 0), "\n")
cat("Max facilities in one block group:", max(cbg_proj$facility_count), "\n")
```


# Step 7: Buffer Analysis (1-Mile Proximity Zone)

Create 1-mile buffer zones around each facility. Block groups that intersect a buffer are considered to be in a "proximity exposure zone" — regardless of whether a facility falls directly within their boundary.

```{r}
#| label: step.07-buffer

# 1 mile = 5280 feet (EPSG:2868 units are US survey feet)
buffer_dist_ft <- 5280

# Create 1-mile buffers around each facility
facility_buffers <- st_buffer(facilities_proj, dist = buffer_dist_ft)

# Dissolve overlapping buffers into a single combined zone
buffer_union <- st_union(facility_buffers)

# Identify CBGs that intersect the buffer zone (binary: in/out)
cbg_proj$near_facility <- as.integer(
  lengths(st_intersects(cbg_proj, buffer_union)) > 0
)

cat("Block groups within 1 mile of any facility:",
    sum(cbg_proj$near_facility), "\n")
cat("Percent of all Maricopa CBGs:",
    round(100 * mean(cbg_proj$near_facility), 1), "%\n")
```

```{r}
#| label: step.07-buffer-map

cbg_wgs84   <- st_transform(cbg_proj, 4326)
buf_wgs84   <- st_transform(buffer_union, 4326)
fac_wgs84   <- st_transform(facilities_proj, 4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = cbg_wgs84,
          aes(fill = factor(near_facility)), color = NA, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "#cccccc", "1" = "#e53935"),
                    labels = c("Outside buffer", "Within 1-mile buffer"),
                    name = "Proximity") +
  geom_sf(data = buf_wgs84, fill = NA, color = "#e53935",
          linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_sf(data = fac_wgs84, color = "#1a1a1a", size = 3, shape = 16) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "1-Mile Buffer Zones Around EPA Facilities",
       subtitle = "Maricopa County Block Groups | Red = within 1-mile exposure zone",
       caption = "Buffer distance: 1 mile (5,280 ft) | CRS: EPSG:2868 → displayed in WGS84")
```


# Step 8: Enriched Hardship Index

Add the two new spatial variables — `facility_count` and `near_facility` — to the hardship index as additional indicators of neighborhood disadvantage. Re-run Cronbach's alpha to assess whether environmental burden hangs together with census-based hardship.

```{r}
#| label: step.08-enriched

# Build the enriched index: standardize new spatial variables
spatial_vars <- cbg_proj |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(GEOID, facility_count, near_facility)

# Merge spatial variables back to the index data
cbg_enriched_data <- cbg_proj |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(GEOID) |>
  bind_cols(index_z |> select(-hardship_index)) |>
  left_join(spatial_vars, by = "GEOID") |>
  mutate(
    facility_z   = as.numeric(scale(facility_count)),
    near_fac_z   = as.numeric(scale(near_facility))
  )

# 7-variable enriched index (5 census + 2 spatial)
enriched_vars <- cbg_enriched_data |>
  select(poverty_rate, unemp_rate, no_hs_rate, no_ins_rate,
         median_income, facility_z, near_fac_z)

alpha_enriched <- psych::alpha(enriched_vars, check.keys = TRUE)
alpha_val_enrich <- round(alpha_enriched$total$raw_alpha, 3)

cat("Original 5-variable alpha:", round(alpha_original$total$raw_alpha, 3), "\n")
cat("Enriched 7-variable alpha:", alpha_val_enrich, "\n")
cat("Change:", round(alpha_val_enrich - alpha_original$total$raw_alpha, 3), "\n")
```

```{r}
#| label: step.08-index

cbg_proj$hardship_enriched <- rowMeans(enriched_vars, na.rm = TRUE)
```


# Step 9: Comparative Maps

Map the original 5-variable and enriched 7-variable indices side by side. The difference map highlights where spatial integration changes the picture.

```{r}
#| label: step.09-maps
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 6

cbg_map <- st_transform(cbg_proj, 4326)

p_orig <- ggplot(cbg_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hardship_index), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno", name = "Hardship\n(Original)",
                       na.value = "grey80") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Original 5-Variable Index",
       subtitle = "Census variables only")

p_enrich <- ggplot(cbg_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hardship_enriched), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno", name = "Hardship\n(Enriched)",
                       na.value = "grey80") +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Enriched 7-Variable Index",
       subtitle = "Census + EPA facility proximity")

p_orig + p_enrich +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Does Spatial Integration Change the Picture?",
    caption = "ACS 5-Year (2023) + EPA TRI facility data | Maricopa County Block Groups"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: step.09-difference
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6

cbg_map <- cbg_map |>
  mutate(index_diff = hardship_enriched - hardship_index)

ggplot(cbg_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = index_diff), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#4575b4",
    mid      = "#f7f7f7",
    high     = "#d73027",
    midpoint = 0,
    name     = "Enriched\nminus\nOriginal"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(
    title    = "Difference: Enriched − Original Index",
    subtitle = "Red = worse when spatial burden included | Blue = better",
    caption  = "ACS 5-Year (2023) + EPA TRI data"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: save-datasets

saveRDS(cbg_proj, "cbg_lab3_complete.rds")
cat("Saved: cbg_lab3_complete.rds\n")
cat("Contains: hardship_index, hardship_enriched, facility_count, near_facility, tract_id\n")
```

---

::: {.callout-note}
## Ready for the assignment?
Head to the [Lab 3 Assignment page](https://antjam-howell.github.io/paf-516-labs/lab3-assignment.html) to download `Lab3_Assignment.qmd`. You will modify the buffer distance, interpret the ICC result, and evaluate whether spatial integration improves the hardship index.
:::
